item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this. I really liked the list of alien creatures found there. They are all interesting, creative, and rightfully confined within a short list that doesn't bog down the article. I also found the ending to be weirdly heartwarming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, love when the foundation emphasizes the P in SCP.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The remaining image could not be sourced and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda dissapointed at the fact that you mentioned aerial exploration, but do not even hint at what is found if flying over the walls. other than that, it's a cool scp

item.title: Re: I think this is a necropost.
item.content:encoded:
On rereading, this post from five years ago

Yes, I'm pretty sure they're aware.

item.title: I think this is a necropost.
item.content:encoded: Er.. you do realize you replied to a comment 5 years and 19 days [or so] old right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On rereading, this post from five years ago sums up my thoughts pretty damn concisely. I'll keep my upvote but I feel like the note at the end was… well, bad writing. This could be smoother.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "They aren't simply some kind of dangerous predator. We said that to keep personnel away from them."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is one thing that bothers me here though. If they think of the foundation as Gods, then why are they attacking Foundation personell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one of favorite SCPs it's really cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: Improving the end of the article
item.content:encoded:
The article started out fairly interesting - I'm always happy to hear a little more about MC&D. Two points:

1. The -7 entry for the sapient "reptomammal" feels like an awkward tone shift from the other records in the table of fauna. Perhaps make it more clinical and brief, or spice up some of the other entries to even it out.

2. The ending note tries to push the article over the top, but feels flat. What if instead of that note we had documentation of ephemera, species, and other observations from the Foundation's exploration? It could include mundane photos of some of the flora/fauna, and documentation of the cave paintings - including the one with the logo. Also, you could then tuck away the photo of the reptomammal in the collection. That way we can imagine it first, before seeing the image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got hung up on that point as well, Litfried. Especially the redaction, it's hard to imagine why a rating on the Mohs scale would require redaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice idea, and it's nice to see the Foundation get some appreciation for all their sterling work.

Problem:

as-of-yet unidentified mineral rating up to ███ on the Mohs scale.

The Mohs scale basically only goes up to 10. It's not an absolute scale where, say, 4 is twice as hard as 2 (consider using the Vickers scale for such), it's entirely relative. By stating it's harder than diamond, you've already said that it is 10+, which is the highest rating on the scale. So a) it doesn't make sense to redact it; b) you can't get three-figure Mohs values.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see the Foundation do unintentionally heroic things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author has to be convincing (and consistent) enough to sell their idea, and the reader needs to have a flexible enough imagination to accept the breaks from reality that the author creates. It's entirely a team effort lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh, what?

Suspension of disbelief is absolutely the author's job. In any sort of fiction, the moment your reader ceases to believe that in the world the glimpse of which your work is showing, under the rules that you shown it to operate, the events you are writing about would have plausibly happened is the moment your piece of fiction has failed, at least where the reader is concerned, because it's very, very difficult to invoke any sort of emotion in a reader or watcher once it is broken.

It doesn't matter whether it's an awful-looking picture, a failure to keep by the estabilished rules, implausible attempts at explanation (aka techno-babble) or characters acting in ways no real person in their situation would. It's still the same old suspension of disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding what Rumetzen said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What? No. It's a team effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Suspension of disbelief is the reader's job, not the author's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the picture of the -7 actually detracts from the article since it's obviously a painting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, it does need cleanup, but the concept is awesome.
Not sure whether to upvote or neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but the tone's a bit off. One thing that really killed my immersion was that you expunged the lizards' description when there's a picture right there. Weirdly enough, they're almost exactly what I pictured 682 as.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't mind the -7 expungements at all - after all, there is a lot of expunged articles here I am very interested in, except they are, well, expunged and what will they contain, we'll never know.

What got me interested was the signs on the walls and the worship sign. At first, I thought - could it mean MCD liberating the universe from some kind of human hunter presence there, and some kind of a MCD symbol? Strange.

…In a second I realised it was the foundation who liberated the universe from MCD guys.

Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminded me of SCP-1000 a bit, but kind of on the opposite spectrum. Whereas SCP-1000 were basically the gods to us before we destroyed them, we're kind of the gods to SCP-1660-7.

Also, just a request to the author. If you don't mind telling me where you got the SCP-1660-7 picture from, it's really cool and I would love to know it's original context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really like this article, certainly remind me scp-1000, but i think it works good with this. Also i tought that normal animals between new species (including 1660-7) was a bite hard to match. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"crude humanoid figures holding sticks and killing with them at a
distance, shooting projectiles." = MC&D clients

"a battle with the same sticks, new figures, and the end of the killing. " = the Foundation comes in and puts an end to all that bullshit.

Legends have to start somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, my first impression about the -7's was the same as yours, but when I re-read it I realized that I had badly misinterpreted something. The -7's didn't know about us in advance, and the paintings aren't very old. On the contrary, the paintings with our logo on them are extremely new; they're recording the Foundation as their saviors from MC&D in response to it happening right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last note is too melodramatic, and the whole thing with lizards reminds me of 1000 too much.

Designations don't make much sense. Designating all flora as 1660-A and fauna as 1660-B, and then designating individual species as A-1, A-2, etc. would look better, I think.

The species themselves are a mess. There are normal animals, normal but somewhat different for no reason animals, and giant awesome lizards, and all of this don't have any resemblance of internal sense or unifying theme or anything.

Connection to MC&D doesn't really add much. Also, the tag for MC&D is missing.

The picture of -7 is really awesome and the rest of the article just isn't as good in comparison.

The last note is melodramatic, again, and also don't make much sense. The addition of the Foundation symbol seems like "Boooo! They know about us! And there is some giant and ancient mystery hidden here, with the very existence of ours at its core!" sort of thing.

The description of 1660-2 is good, novel, and well thought-out, except for the thing that Khym Chanur said above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i very much like the idea, but DEAR GOD does this need proofreading, editing, and general cleanup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We could rule them like gods! …evil gods
…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loads of edits made based on your comments. How's it doing now?

Also, Edrobot now I'm tempted to change this from "unearthly forest" to "pocket universe full of neanderthal lizardmen that worship the Foundation as gods" or perhaps just "neanderthal lizardmen."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture makes it for me. Just that thing…looking at you.

Everybody else has already given what I would: remove all the [DATA EXPUNGED] in the description. SCP-1000 has a similar idea, but rather than blocking out the info, they just give false information. I would suggest saying that the creatures are just like the rest, rather than blocking out everything.

One thing nobody else has mentioned-in the note, why is there that awkward jump between "on" and "bioluminescent"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting concept. Love the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I think the author meant "cougar". There are a couple of other problems with that sentence, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Judging from that note, the neanderthal lizardmen didn't start worshiping the Foundation until the SCP was rescued from MC&D. The question then becomes, how were they aware of what was going on outside the gate, and what else do they know about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Specimen resembles a conger or other type of big cat apart from lacking eyes, forward extended ratlike ears and the ability to echo-locate.

This is a conger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whats especially interesting is that even though the sapient reptiles worship the Foundation, the SCP was in the hands of MDC. Really makes you wonder about the history of the item.

Upon ignition the fire will, regardless of materials used, begin to emit large amounts of smoke which will begin to gather forming an arch shaped "gate" in the air approximately ███ meters across.

A gate at least 100 meters across. So what happens if it's inside a room while it's emitting smoke? Will the smoke try to escape to an open space, or will the gate shrink to fit the confines of the room?

SCP-1660-2 is a miniature parallel universe consisting of

  1. How do they know it's miniature, since they can't get out of the confines of the "box"? There might be stuff beyond the indestructible material. If instead of a box, going too far in one direction brought you back to your starting place, then they could conclude that it was indeed small.
  2. How do they know it's another universe, as opposed to being some location within this universe?
  3. If it is a different universe, why is it "parallel"? Because most of the species are similar to Earth species?

Specimens have been seen to reach sizes of up to ██ millimeters. How they are able to breath via spiracles while said sizes is unknown.

Large dragonflies from prehistoric times were able to live due to the higher oxygen content of the air, so the article should probably mention that the atmosphere has the same makeup as present day Earth.

SCP-1660-7 unidentified moss

That should be SCP-1660-8, since -7 is already used by the sapient species.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The very last sentence is a little ham-handed but I like where you're going with this. Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. The classified note did it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, guys, if you see typos, you're allowed to go in and fix them… in fact, the Description should be considerably easier to read now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted with reservations. Get rid or improve on the expungements in the notes on 1660-7. And to me, the classified note on 1660-7 reads a bit… I don't know the right word for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really anything to say that hasn't already been said, so I'll make this quick: I like the idea. Keep working on it and it can be something awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have two different things listed as SCP-1660-7. Which ,arguably was kinda cool when for a while, I thought the moss is sapient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is a pocket universe full of neanderthal lizardmen that worship the Foundation as gods?

…Somehow that sounds pretty cool. +1

Also, "Neanderthal Lizardmen" is a pretty good band name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First complaint is that there are so many typos, second is that's obviously a nautilus shell, third is the tone of that classified note. It feels more like talking to the reader rather than any sort of in-universe communique.

That said, the SCP-1660-7 picture works really well with this (except the moss is also labelled SCP-1660-7). It just needs some cleaning up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can convey that there is something we're not supposed to know about 1660-7 without peppering so many [DATA EXPUNGED]s in there. It looks hokey. The classified bit was interesting, however.

I like that this is a pocket reality SCP that's not so ambitious as to include fifty different exploration logs. No MTF whateverthefuck task members going, "it looks like a bird sir. WITH TEETH." You cut through the potential chaff and get to the hook quickly. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No offense, but I laughed my ass off reading that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted because it needs some work. I also don't understand why we can't decode their light language when we can do an approximate job of understanding cuttlefish and squid signals.

emit large amounts of smoke while will begin to gather forming

This is a good example of why this needs work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as said before, spelling and grammar need a bit work. Also I really feel like the classified note should be written a bit more clerical, it kind of reads right now as a "Dun dun dunnnn!" type of paragraph rather then a "This is something we know and want to keep secret". Final thing is i think you could get rid of a couple of the expunges and replace it with a lie or half-truth like SCP-1000 tends to do.

I really like everything else, neutral for now though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an interesting take on a pocket universe, but the containment procedures need a lot of work and it needs some spelling and grammar cleanup.

No vote for now, clean it up a bit and I'll take another look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was pissed by the expungement until I read the classified note, then I was cool with it. I really like the idea here, but the execution could be tightened up a bit.
